concepts.headline: Essential books for product builders.
concepts.subtext: Organized by jobs-to-be-done. Three books per category, all completed, all high signal.
data_points: Strategy — understand competitive dynamics and positioning
Leadership — build influence and develop teams
Execution — ship faster and manage complexity
User insight — understand what customers actually want
Systems thinking — see patterns, not just problems
concepts.layout_description: Stacked list layout with left-anchored heading block. Category rows use square number markers in warm sand accent. Each row shows category name left-aligned with a short descriptor in secondary gray. FLYWHEEL watermark behind at opacity 0.03. Particle dots clustered in upper-right corner.

layout_spec.accent_placement: thin 2px warm sand bar beneath headline, fading right

concepts.color_usage: Black #000 for headline and category names. #6b7a7c for descriptors. #d7c8af for square number markers and accent bar. #ffffff background.
concepts.reasoning: The post is explicitly structured as a categorized list with a clear taxonomy. A numbered list graphic with named categories maps directly to the jobs-to-be-done framing, letting the structure of the content be the visual.
visualization_goals: Show the curated book collection organized by jobs-to-be-done categories with exactly 3 books per category
Highlight the proportion of classic (10+ years old) versus newer books in the collection to reflect the Andreessen philosophy
Visualize the curation funnel: books considered vs. books read vs. books that made the final high-signal list
Map each book category to common product manager development areas or skill gaps it addresses

concepts.headline: Most great books are already old.
concepts.subtext: The best product reading list skews heavily toward books over ten years old, per the Andreessen principle.
concepts.layout_description: Left-anchored hero layout. Headline and subtext occupy the left 50% of the canvas. Donut chart rendered in the right 40%, vertically centered, using black and warm sand as the two segment colors. Center of donut shows '78%' in DM Mono. Legend below chart in secondary gray. Particle network dots scattered at the upper-right periphery at low opacity.

layout_spec.accent_placement: 2px warm sand bar above headline label

concepts.color_usage: Segment one #000000 for classics. Segment two #d7c8af for newer titles. Center stat in DM Mono #000. Subtext in #6b7a7c. #ffffff background.
concepts.reasoning: The Andreessen philosophy about reading older books is the most intellectually distinctive point in the post. Visualizing the classics-to-newer ratio makes the curation principle immediately legible and gives the graphic a distinct point of view that invites engagement.
visualization_goals: Show the curated book collection organized by jobs-to-be-done categories with exactly 3 books per category
Highlight the proportion of classic (10+ years old) versus newer books in the collection to reflect the Andreessen philosophy
Visualize the curation funnel: books considered vs. books read vs. books that made the final high-signal list
Map each book category to common product manager development areas or skill gaps it addresses

concepts.headline: Read books that have stood the test of time.
concepts.subtext: The best reading list for product builders skews toward classics. High signal, no noise.
concepts.layout_description: Centered statement layout. Headline rendered at 72px weight 600 occupying the upper-center of the canvas. Subtext in 16px secondary gray below with generous vertical gap. Thin 2px warm sand accent line beneath the headline, 120px wide, left-offset from center. FLYWHEEL watermark at extreme size behind at opacity 0.03. Particle network in lower-right quadrant at opacity 0.08.

layout_spec.accent_placement: 2px warm sand underline beneath headline, left-offset

concepts.color_usage: Headline #000000. Subtext #6b7a7c. Accent bar #d7c8af. Background #ffffff. Watermark #000000 at opacity 0.03.
concepts.reasoning: The Andreessen insight is the most quotable, memorable idea in the post. A bold statement card isolates it as the single focal element, rewarding readers who pause, and creates natural discussion in comments about reading philosophy.
visualization_goals: Show the curated book collection organized by jobs-to-be-done categories with exactly 3 books per category
Highlight the proportion of classic (10+ years old) versus newer books in the collection to reflect the Andreessen philosophy
Visualize the curation funnel: books considered vs. books read vs. books that made the final high-signal list
Map each book category to common product manager development areas or skill gaps it addresses

selection_reasoning: The donut chart concept is the strongest because it converts the most intellectually specific and differentiating claim in the post (the Andreessen principle about reading old books) into a concrete, visual fact. It gives readers something to react to, a real proportion, not just a sentiment. The left-right composition with the headline anchoring the left and the chart occupying the right creates an asymmetric editorial tension that aligns with Flywheel's Swiss-design identity. It also avoids the generic 'list of categories' treatment that most productivity posts use, instead asserting a point of view through data.
